preamble.summary: The book opens with a superscription identifying Qohelet ('the Assembler') as a son of David and king in Jerusalem. What follows is the thesis statement of the entire work: hevel havalim — 'vapor of vapors' — everything is vapor. Qohelet then launches into a poem on the wearying cycles of nature (sun, wind, streams) to argue that nothing under the sun is genuinely new. The chapter closes with Qohelet's autobiographical introduction: he applied his mind to investigate everything done under heaven and found it all to be a chasing after wind.
preamble.remarkable: The word hevel appears five times in verse 2 alone and will appear thirty-eight times across the book. Its traditional rendering as 'vanity' (KJV) or 'meaningless' (NIV) has profoundly shaped Western readings of Ecclesiastes as a work of nihilism or despair. But hevel literally means 'breath' or 'vapor' — something that exists, that you can see on a cold morning, but that you cannot hold or keep. It is not 'nothing' but 'nothing lasting.' This distinction matters enormously. Qohelet is not saying life is pointless; he is saying life is transient, elusive, and impossible to grip. The Covenant Rendering uses 'vapor' throughout to preserve this concrete, physical image. The nature poem in verses 4-7 is not decorative but argumentative: the sun rises and sets, the wind circles, the rivers flow to the sea — and nothing changes. The world is a closed loop. This is Qohelet's evidence for his thesis.
preamble.friction: The superscription's claim that Qohelet is 'son of David, king in Jerusalem' has traditionally been read as identifying Solomon, but the book's late Hebrew vocabulary, Aramaic loan-words, and Persian-period syntax make Solomonic authorship impossible on linguistic grounds. The Solomonic fiction is a literary device: who better to test whether wisdom, wealth, and achievement can provide lasting satisfaction than the king who had all three? Qohelet is a persona, not a historical claim. The word qohelet itself is a feminine participle from qahal ('to assemble'), used as a title rather than a name — something like 'the one who convenes the assembly' or simply 'the Assembler.'
preamble.connections: Qohelet's investigation of wisdom echoes Proverbs 1:1-7 but reaches radically different conclusions — where Proverbs promises that wisdom yields life, Qohelet finds that wisdom yields grief (v. 18). The nature poem shares imagery with Psalm 19:4-6 (the sun's circuit) but strips it of all praise. The phrase 'under the sun' (tachat ha-shemesh) appears twenty-nine times in Ecclesiastes and nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible — it is Qohelet's signature frame, limiting his inquiry to what can be observed in the natural world without appeal to revelation or eschatology.

verses.text_kjv: The words of the Preacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem.
verses.rendering: The words of Qohelet, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
translator_notes: Qohelet is not a name but a title derived from qahal ('to assemble, to gather'). The form is a feminine participle used as a professional designation — 'the Assembler' or 'the Convener.' We retain the Hebrew title rather than translating it as 'Preacher' (KJV) or 'Teacher' (NIV), since neither captures the original sense. The Solomonic framing ('son of David, king in Jerusalem') establishes the literary persona through which the book conducts its philosophical experiment.

key_terms.semantic_range: assembler, convener, gatherer; one who addresses an assembly
key_terms.note: From qahal ('to assemble'). The feminine participle form used as a title is unusual and has no exact parallel in Hebrew. It functions like a professional role — 'the one who gathers' — whether gathering people for instruction or gathering observations about life.

verses.text_hebrew: הֲבֵ֤ל הֲבָלִים֙ אָמַ֣ר קֹהֶ֔לֶת הֲבֵ֥ל הֲבָלִ֖ים הַכֹּ֥ל הָֽבֶל׃
verses.text_kjv: Vanity of vanities, saith the Preacher, vanity of vanities; all is vanity.
verses.rendering:
Vapor of vapors, says Qohelet.
Vapor of vapors — everything is vapor.
verses.expanded_rendering: The Hebrew hevel havalim is a superlative construction — 'vapor of vapors' means 'the most vapor-like thing imaginable,' just as 'song of songs' means the greatest song and 'holy of holies' means the most sacred place. Qohelet repeats the phrase twice and then universalizes it: ha-kol havel, 'everything is vapor.' This is the thesis statement of the entire book. Hevel appears five times in this single verse — an intensity that signals this is not a passing observation but a programmatic declaration. The word hevel literally means 'breath' or 'vapor' — Abel's name (Hevel) in Genesis carries the same word, and Abel's brief, extinguished life embodies the concept. Qohelet is not declaring that life is meaningless or absurd; he is declaring that life is transient, ungraspable, and ephemeral. You can see vapor but you cannot hold it. That is the human condition as Qohelet sees it.
translator_notes: The rendering 'vapor' rather than 'vanity' (KJV) or 'meaningless' (NIV) recovers the physical concreteness of the Hebrew. Hevel is not an abstract philosophical concept but a sensory image — breath on a cold day, morning mist, steam rising from a pot. The word does not negate existence; it characterizes its duration and graspability.

The superlative construction hevel havalim appears only here and in 12:8, forming an inclusio that frames the entire book. Everything between these two declarations is contained within the thesis of vapor.

key_terms.semantic_range: breath, vapor, mist, futility, transience, ephemerality; something insubstantial and fleeting
key_terms.note: Hevel is the signature word of Ecclesiastes, appearing 38 times across 12 chapters. Its literal meaning is 'breath' or 'vapor' — the visible but ungraspable exhalation on a cold morning. The KJV's 'vanity' imports Latin philosophical categories (vanitas) that flatten the Hebrew into moral judgment. The NIV's 'meaningless' turns Qohelet into a nihilist, which he is not. 'Vapor' preserves the physical image and its implications: real but transient, visible but ungraspable, present but passing. This is the controlling metaphor of the entire book.

verses.text_hebrew: מַה־יִּתְר֖וֹן לָֽאָדָ֑ם בְּכׇל־עֲמָל֔וֹ שֶֽׁיַּעֲמֹ֖ל תַּ֥חַת הַשָּֽׁמֶשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: What profit hath a man of all his labour which he taketh under the sun?
verses.rendering:
What lasting gain does a person have
from all the toil at which he toils under the sun?
translator_notes: The word yitron ('profit, advantage, gain') is a commercial term — literally 'what is left over.' Qohelet frames life as an accounting problem: after all the effort, what is the net surplus? The implied answer is: nothing lasting. The phrase tachat ha-shemesh ('under the sun') appears here for the first time and will recur twenty-nine times. It establishes the boundaries of Qohelet's investigation: he is examining life as it can be observed in the visible world, without appeal to afterlife or divine revelation.

key_terms.semantic_range: profit, surplus, advantage, what remains; from yatar ('to be left over, to remain')
key_terms.note: A commercial term unique to Ecclesiastes in the Hebrew Bible. Qohelet asks whether human labor produces any net surplus — anything that outlasts the effort itself. The answer, consistently, is no.

key_terms.semantic_range: beneath the sun; in the visible, earthly realm
key_terms.note: Qohelet's signature phrase, appearing 29 times and found nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible. It limits his investigation to empirical observation — what can be seen and measured in the world of human experience.

verses.text_hebrew: דּ֤וֹר הֹלֵךְ֙ וְד֣וֹר בָּ֔א וְהָאָ֖רֶץ לְעוֹלָ֥ם עֹמָֽדֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: One generation passeth away, and another generation cometh: but the earth abideth for ever.
verses.rendering:
A generation goes and a generation comes,
but the earth stands forever.
translator_notes: The poem on nature's cycles begins. Generations pass but the stage remains. The word olam ('forever, perpetuity') applied to the earth contrasts sharply with the transience of human life. The earth is the permanent backdrop against which the brief drama of each generation plays out and is forgotten.

verses.text_hebrew: וְזָרַ֥ח הַשֶּׁ֖מֶשׁ וּבָ֣א הַשָּׁ֑מֶשׁ וְאֶ֨ל־מְקוֹמ֔וֹ שׁוֹאֵ֛ף זוֹרֵ֥חַ ה֖וּא שָֽׁם׃
verses.text_kjv: The sun also ariseth, and the sun goeth down, and hasteth to his place where he arose.
verses.rendering:
The sun rises and the sun sets,
then it pants back to the place where it rises again.
translator_notes: The verb sho'ef ('pants, gasps, hastens') personifies the sun as a runner straining to complete its circuit. This is not a joyful image like Psalm 19:5, where the sun is a bridegroom running with gladness — here the sun is exhausted, gasping its way back to the starting line only to run the same course again. The word choice turns the solar cycle from wonder into weariness.

verses.text_hebrew: הוֹלֵךְ֙ אֶל־דָּר֔וֹם וְסוֹבֵ֖ב אֶל־צָפ֑וֹן סוֹבֵ֤ב ׀ סֹבֵב֙ הוֹלֵ֣ךְ הָר֔וּחַ וְעַל־סְבִיבֹתָ֖יו שָׁ֥ב הָרֽוּחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: The wind goeth toward the south, and turneth about unto the north; it whirleth about continually, and the wind returneth again according to his circuits.
verses.rendering:
Going south, then turning north —
round and round the wind goes,
and on its circuits the wind returns.
translator_notes: The repetition of sovev ('turning, circling') three times in a single verse mimics the wind's endless circling through sound. The word ruach means both 'wind' and 'spirit' — an ambiguity Qohelet will exploit throughout the book. Here the wind's aimless cycling is exhibit B in the case for cosmic monotony.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־הַנְּחָלִ֞ים הֹלְכִ֣ים אֶל־הַיָּ֗ם וְהַיָּם֙ אֵינֶ֣נּוּ מָלֵ֔א אֶל־מְק֗וֹם שֶׁ֤הַנְּחָלִים֙ הֹֽלְכִ֔ים שָׁ֖ם הֵ֥ם שָׁבִ֖ים לָלָֽכֶת׃
verses.text_kjv: All the rivers run into the sea; yet the sea is not full; unto the place from whence the rivers come, thither they return again.
verses.rendering:
All the streams flow to the sea,
but the sea is never full.
To the place where the streams flow,
there they return to flow again.
translator_notes: The streams complete the triad: sun, wind, water — all moving ceaselessly, arriving nowhere new. The sea's refusal to fill despite endless inflow is the poem's most striking image of futility. Effort without accumulation. Motion without progress. The streams do not fail to reach the sea; they reach it and it makes no difference.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־הַדְּבָרִ֣ים יְגֵעִ֔ים לֹא־יוּכַ֥ל אִ֖ישׁ לְדַבֵּ֑ר לֹא־תִשְׂבַּ֥ע עַ֙יִן֙ לִרְא֔וֹת וְלֹא־תִמָּלֵ֥א אֹ֖זֶן מִשְּׁמֹֽעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: All things are full of labour; man cannot utter it: the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing.
verses.rendering:
All things are wearisome —
more than anyone can express.
The eye is not satisfied with seeing,
nor the ear filled with hearing.
translator_notes: The word yege'im ('wearisome, toilsome') applies to devarim, which means both 'things' and 'words.' The ambiguity is deliberate: all things are wearying, and all words fail to capture it. The eye and ear — the primary channels of human perception — are insatiable. This is not a celebration of human curiosity but an indictment: we keep looking and listening and are never filled.

verses.text_hebrew: מַה־שֶּֽׁהָיָה֙ ה֣וּא שֶׁיִּהְיֶ֔ה וּמַה־שֶׁנַּֽעֲשָׂ֔ה ה֖וּא שֶׁיֵּעָשֶׂ֑ה וְאֵ֥ין כׇּל־חָדָ֖שׁ תַּ֥חַת הַשָּֽׁמֶשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: The thing that hath been, it is that which shall be; and that which is done is that which shall be done: and there is no new thing under the sun.
verses.rendering:
What has been is what will be,
and what has been done is what will be done.
There is nothing new under the sun.
translator_notes: This is Qohelet's most famous line and the conclusion drawn from the nature poem. The parallel structure is perfectly symmetrical: what was / will be, what was done / will be done. The denial of novelty is radical — it challenges every human claim to innovation, discovery, and progress. Ein kol chadash tachat ha-shemesh: 'there is nothing at all new under the sun.'

verses.text_hebrew: יֵ֥שׁ דָּבָ֛ר שֶׁיֹּאמַ֥ר רְאֵה־זֶ֖ה חָדָ֣שׁ ה֑וּא כְּבָר֙ הָיָ֣ה לְעֹֽלָמִ֔ים אֲשֶׁ֥ר הָיָ֖ה מִלְּפָנֵֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Is there any thing whereof it may be said, See, this is new? it hath been already of old time, which was before us.
verses.rendering: Is there anything of which someone might say, 'Look, this is new'? It has already existed in ages past, in times before us.
translator_notes: Qohelet anticipates the objection and dismisses it. The rhetorical question expects 'no' as its answer. The phrase le-olamim ('for ages, in antiquity') pushes the denial of novelty back into deep time. Whatever seems new merely seems so because of failed memory.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֥ין זִכְר֖וֹן לָרִֽאשֹׁנִ֑ים וְגַ֨ם לָאַחֲרֹנִ֜ים שֶׁיִּהְי֗וּ לֹֽא־יִהְיֶ֤ה לָהֶם֙ זִכָּר֔וֹן עִ֥ם שֶׁיִּהְי֖וּ לָאַחֲרֹנָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: There is no remembrance of former things; neither shall there be any remembrance of things that are to come with those that shall come after.
verses.rendering:
There is no remembrance of those who came before,
and even those yet to come will not be remembered
by those who follow after them.
translator_notes: The poem ends by diagnosing why the illusion of novelty persists: memory fails. The rishonim ('former ones') are forgotten, and the acharonim ('later ones') will be too. Each generation lives in the illusion that its experience is unprecedented because it cannot remember what came before. The loss of memory is not accidental but structural — it is how the cycle perpetuates itself.

verses.text_hebrew: אֲנִ֣י קֹהֶ֗לֶת הָיִ֥יתִי מֶ֛לֶךְ עַל־יִשְׂרָאֵ֖ל בִּירוּשָׁלָֽ͏ִם׃
verses.text_kjv: I the Preacher was king over Israel in Jerusalem.
verses.rendering: I, Qohelet, was king over Israel in Jerusalem.
translator_notes: The shift to first person marks the transition from the editor's frame (vv. 1-11) to Qohelet's own voice. The past tense 'was king' (hayiti melekh) is striking — it suggests looking back on kingship as a completed experience, which complicates the Solomonic fiction since Solomon reigned until death. The autobiographical section now begins.

verses.text_hebrew: וְנָתַ֣תִּי אֶת־לִבִּ֗י לִדְר֤וֹשׁ וְלָתוּר֙ בַּֽחׇכְמָ֔ה עַ֛ל כׇּל־אֲשֶׁ֥ר נַעֲשָׂ֖ה תַּ֣חַת הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם ה֤וּא עִנְיַן־רָע֙ נָתַ֣ן אֱלֹהִ֔ים לִבְנֵ֥י הָאָדָ֖ם לַעֲנ֥וֹת בּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And I gave my heart to seek and search out by wisdom concerning all things that are done under heaven: it is a sore travail hath God given to the sons of man to be exercised therewith.
verses.rendering: I set my mind to investigate and explore by wisdom everything done under heaven. It is a grievous task God has given to human beings to be occupied with.
verses.expanded_rendering: Qohelet describes his method: he 'gave his heart' (natan et-libbo) — the heart (lev) in Hebrew being the seat of intellect, not emotion — to 'seek' (darash, systematic investigation) and 'explore' (tur, a surveying, reconnaissance term used of the spies in Numbers 13). His tools are wisdom (chokmah) and his scope is everything done under heaven. But the result is not satisfying discovery — it is inyan ra, 'a grievous occupation' or 'a miserable task.' The word inyan ('business, occupation, task') appears only in Ecclesiastes in the Hebrew Bible. God himself is the one who gave (natan) this burdensome occupation to humanity. This is a startling claim: the compulsion to investigate life is divinely imposed but leads only to frustration.
translator_notes: The verb tur ('to explore, to spy out') is the same word used when Moses sent spies to 'tour' the land of Canaan (Numbers 13:2). Qohelet is conducting a reconnaissance of reality, surveying the terrain of human experience. The military-intelligence connotation adds urgency and purpose to what might otherwise seem like idle philosophizing.

The phrase inyan ra ('grievous task') frames the human drive to understand as a burden, not a gift. God assigned it, and it produces sorrow. This tension between divine sovereignty and human frustration runs through the entire book.

key_terms.note: A word found exclusively in Ecclesiastes within the Hebrew Bible. It may derive from the root anah ('to be occupied, to be busy') and carries the sense of a preoccupation that weighs on the mind. Qohelet uses it eight times to describe the burdensome occupations God assigns to humanity.

verses.text_hebrew: רָאִ֗יתִי אֶת־כׇּל־הַֽמַּעֲשִׂ֛ים שֶׁנַּעֲשׂ֖וּ תַּ֣חַת הַשָּׁ֑מֶשׁ וְהִנֵּ֥ה הַכֹּ֛ל הֶ֖בֶל וּרְע֥וּת רֽוּחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: I have seen all the works that are done under the sun; and, behold, all is vanity and vexation of spirit.
verses.rendering: I have observed all the deeds done under the sun, and look — everything is vapor and a chasing after wind.
translator_notes: The phrase re'ut ruach ('chasing after wind' or 'shepherding wind') appears nine times in Ecclesiastes. The word re'ut may derive from ra'ah ('to shepherd, to feed') or from ra'ah ('to desire, to strive after'). Either reading yields the same image: trying to herd the wind or grasping at it — an exercise in futility. The pairing of hevel ('vapor') with re'ut ruach ('chasing wind') creates Qohelet's most characteristic expression: life is both insubstantial and ungraspable.

key_terms.semantic_range: shepherding wind, striving after wind, desire of spirit, feeding on wind
key_terms.note: Qohelet's signature companion phrase to hevel. Whether derived from 'to shepherd' or 'to desire,' the image is the same: wind cannot be caught, directed, or possessed. Every human effort to achieve lasting significance is like trying to herd the wind.

verses.text_hebrew: מְעֻוָּ֖ת לֹא־יוּכַ֣ל לִתְקֹ֑ן וְחֶסְר֖וֹן לֹא־יוּכַ֥ל לְהִמָּנֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: That which is crooked cannot be made straight: and that which is wanting cannot be numbered.
verses.rendering:
What is crooked cannot be straightened,
and what is lacking cannot be counted.
translator_notes: A proverbial couplet summarizing Qohelet's finding. The world contains structural defects (me'uvat, 'twisted, crooked') and deficiencies (chesron, 'lack, deficit') that human effort cannot repair or even quantify. The passive voice suggests these are not accidental but built into the fabric of reality. Wisdom can diagnose the crookedness but cannot fix it.

verses.text_hebrew: דִּבַּ֤רְתִּי אֲנִי֙ עִם־לִבִּ֣י לֵאמֹ֔ר אֲנִ֗י הִנֵּ֨ה הִגְדַּ֤לְתִּי וְהוֹסַ֙פְתִּי֙ חׇכְמָ֔ה עַ֛ל כׇּל־אֲשֶׁר־הָיָ֥ה לְפָנַ֖י עַל־יְרוּשָׁלָ֑͏ִם וְלִבִּ֛י רָאָ֥ה הַרְבֵּ֖ה חׇכְמָ֥ה וָדָֽעַת׃
verses.text_kjv: I communed with mine own heart, saying, Lo, I am come to great estate, and have gotten more wisdom than all they that have been before me in Jerusalem: yea, my heart had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.
verses.rendering: I said to myself, 'Look, I have grown and increased in wisdom beyond anyone who ruled before me over Jerusalem, and my mind has observed much wisdom and knowledge.'
translator_notes: The phrase dibarti ani im-libbi ('I spoke, I, with my heart') is Qohelet's characteristic mode of reflection — internal dialogue. The claim to surpass all predecessors in Jerusalem in wisdom reinforces the Solomonic persona (1 Kings 4:29-31 attributes unmatched wisdom to Solomon). But the claim is set up only to be undercut in the next two verses.

verses.text_hebrew: וָאֶתְּנָ֣ה לִבִּ֗י לָדַ֤עַת חׇכְמָה֙ וְדַ֣עַת הוֹלֵל֣וֹת וְשִׂכְל֔וּת יָדַ֕עְתִּי שֶׁגַּם־זֶ֥ה ה֖וּא רַעְי֥וֹן רֽוּחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: And I gave my heart to know wisdom, and to know madness and folly: I perceived that this also is vexation of spirit.
verses.rendering: I set my mind to understand wisdom and to understand madness and folly. I realized that this too is a chasing after wind.
translator_notes: Qohelet's method includes studying the full spectrum — not just wisdom but holelot ('madness, raving') and sikhlut ('folly, senselessness'). He needs the contrast to evaluate wisdom fairly. But even this comprehensive investigation leads to the same verdict: ra'yon ruach ('chasing after wind'), a variant of re'ut ruach in verse 14.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֛י בְּרֹ֥ב חׇכְמָ֖ה רׇב־כָּ֑עַס וְיוֹסִ֥יף דַּ֖עַת יוֹסִ֥יף מַכְאֽוֹב׃
verses.text_kjv: For in much wisdom is much grief: and he that increaseth knowledge increaseth sorrow.
verses.rendering:
For with much wisdom comes much vexation,
and whoever increases knowledge increases pain.
verses.expanded_rendering: The chapter's final verse delivers a devastating counterpoint to the entire wisdom tradition. Proverbs celebrates the acquisition of wisdom as life's highest pursuit; Qohelet agrees that wisdom is real and valuable but insists it comes at a cost. The more you understand about the world's crookedness (v. 15) and the futility of its cycles (vv. 4-7), the more you suffer. Ka'as ('vexation, anger, grief') and makh'ov ('pain, sorrow') are the wages of insight. This is not anti-intellectualism — Qohelet never advises ignorance — but it is wisdom's honest self-assessment. To see clearly is to grieve.
translator_notes: The parallel structure is precise: much wisdom / much vexation, increased knowledge / increased pain. The proportion is direct — wisdom and suffering grow together. This verse will be tested and complicated throughout the book as Qohelet continues to pursue wisdom despite its cost, never abandoning the enterprise he describes as painful.
